person_full_lfm_name: Tong, Jonah Reid Tin Chee Matthew

blurb: One of the better two-way prospects in the 2019 Draft, Faltine lasted until the Red Sox took him in the 39th round because of his commitment to Texas and concerns about his bat. He has become one of college baseball's premier defensive shortstops with the Longhorns but hasn't eased any worries about his offensive game. He posted a .553 OPS with wood bats in the Cape Cod League last summer and though he slammed 15 homers this season, he also struck out 104 times, the second-highest total in NCAA Division I history. Faltine has twitchy actions, plenty of range to both sides and good hands at shortstop. He has a strong arm as well and can make all the throws even if his arm action is somewhat slingy. A rare collegian who's a no-doubt shortstop at the next level, he could play quality defense all over the diamond if he winds up as more of a utilityman than a regular. Most evaluators believe that Faltine will need to overhaul his right-handed swing and his approach in pro ball, and a club that believes that will work could take him in the third round. While he has enough strength and bat speed to provide 15 homers per season, he's too aggressive and pull-happy and his uphill stroke leaves him vulnerable to fastballs up in the zone, not to mention offspeed pitches. An average runner with some savvy on the bases, he draws praise for his makeup.

Maier jumped on the map last summer by pitching well in the Cape Cod League as a former two-way player at the University of British Columbia who then transferred to Oregon. Scouts in the Pacific Northwest were excited to get more looks at him given his lack of track record and he did show a fastball up to 95 and a potentially plus slider, both with excellent spin rates, as well as a solid changeup. But he hurt his elbow, which ended his season after three starts. A team interested in drafting him will do so knowing he’ll have to rehab under pro supervision.

Iadisernia established himself as one of the better college hitters in the Southeast with a strong 2021 season and a banner summer in the Northwoods League, but he slumped to .276/.383/.463 with six homers and 14 steals in 55 games this spring. He still showed a quick left-handed swing and a good approach at the plate. With average power at best and fringy speed and arm strength, he's going to have to depend on his bat to carry him.

Though his well above-average raw power is his carrying tool, Williams also makes consistent contact, controls the strike zone and uses the entire field. A 6-foot-5, 250-pound left-handed hitter whose strength stands out more than his bat speed, he hit .335/.464/.622 with 13 homers in 54 games this spring. He has limited athleticism and must maintain his conditioning to remain at first base.

After losing most of 2020 to the pandemic and much of '21 to a back injury, Metzinger finally broke out this spring, batting .312/427/.591 with 19 homers, 11 steals and 51 walks in 64 games. One of the more attractive fourth-year college prospects, he controls the strike zone and could be an average hitter with power to match as well as solid arm strength and third-base defense.

Morgan played on Team USA’s gold medal-winning 15U team in 2015, a squad featuring current Red Sox prospect Triston Casas, then starred at Lincoln High School in Stockton, Calif., before heading to Fresno State. After redshirting in 2019, he played regularly in the shortened 2020 season, mostly at third, before settling in as the regular catcher a year ago. His Draft stock took a jump when he posted a 1.046 OPS in 2022, and he was named a finalist for the Buster Posey National Collegiate Catcher of the Year Award. The 6-foot backstop gets high marks for being a grinder, one with skills on both sides of the ball. He makes a ton of contact from the right side of the plate, striking out less than seven percent of the time in 2022 while walking nearly 10 percent of the time. He has more extra-base authority than serious home run power, but he did show the ability to drive the ball, with some pop the other way. There is no question Morgan can stick behind the plate, with a solid arm and good receiving skills that were recognized when he was named a finalist for an ABCA/Rawlings Gold Glove. He might profile best as a backup, but with his makeup, developing into a starter isn’t out of the question, giving him a good chance to be the first Bulldogs catcher to go in the first 10 rounds since the Angels made Taylor Ward a first-rounder in 2019.

The Puerto Rico Baseball Academy has produced a fair share of shortstop prospects in the Draft, the most notable one being former No. 1 overall pick Carlos Correa, and most recently Mariners 2021 second-rounder Edwin Arroyo. Another shortstop, Rodriguez will represent the Academy as this year’s best Draft prospect from Puerto Rico, though he is not quite as highly regarded as his aforementioned predecessors. Rodriguez is definitely a glove-over-hit middle infielder right now. His soft and quick hands work very well at short and he has more than enough arm, with good carry on his throws, to stick at the premium position long-term. He’s an above-average runner and should have plenty of range for the spot as well. There are more questions about his offensive profile. The right-handed hitter does have some feel for the barrel and contact, though some scouts don’t love his setup at the plate and he’ll need to refine his approach. He also will need to add strength so he can impact the ball more. Teams will surely be interested in the Alabama-Birmingham recruit’s glove and it will be interesting to see how the hot start of Arroyo, who had a similar “needs to add strength” evaluation, might impact decision-making when it comes to Rodriguez.

Drafted in the 40th round by Cleveland in 2019, Nielson is coming off a successful year pitching out of BYU’s bullpen, and he pitched well as a starter in the Cape Cod League. The lefty has a 90-94 mph fastnall and a hard 77-82 mph slider, while showing some feel for a changeup on the Cape. He took a big step forward in '22 in finding the strike zone, with some scouts believing he’s capable of having solid control.

A fourth-year redshirt sophomore, Palermo sat out the 2019 season and pitched just 26 2/3 innings in the next two years before posting a 2.78 ERA with 74 strikeouts in 58 1/3 frame this spring. He's primarily a one-pitch reliever, working mainly off a 93-95 mph fastball that reaches 99 and features armside run. He has some depth on his mid-80s slider but struggles at times to land it for strikes.

Humphries pitched Fuquay-Varina HS to the North Carolina 4-A state title in 2021, winning the clincher for the Bengals' second championship in three years. He raised his profile with a strong performance at the Super 60 showcase in February and looked like he could be the next in line of recent North Carolina standout prep southpaws that includes MacKenzie Gore, Blake Walston, D.J. Herz, Liam Norris and Josh Hartle. His stuff and strikes haven't been as good during his senior season, though he still could factor into the top three or four rounds. At the Super 60, Humphries worked at 93-95 mph with arm-side run on his fastball and generated high spin rates on a low-80s slider and an upper-70s curveball. This spring, he has sat at 90-93 mph with his heater on his best days and had trouble landing his breaking pitches for strikes. He also has a low-80s changeup with fade that has the potential to become a solid offering. Humphries' control and command have wavered as well this spring. He's not particularly projectable or athletic, and it remains to be seen whether he can maintain quality stuff over a long season to remain a starter. He has committed to Campbell, which has produced a pair of early-round arms in Seth Johnson and Thomas Harrington in the last four years.

McElvain committed to Vanderbilt before throwing his first pitch as a Tennessee high schooler and served as a reliever in his first two college seasons. He became the Commodores' Friday-night starter this spring and worked the first seven innings of a no-hitter against Kentucky on April 22. He mostly struggled afterward before striking out 11 in seven frames against New Mexico State in his final start in the NCAA regionals. McElvain's most effective pitch is his solid 81-85 mph slider with depth, and he also can turn it into a harder 85-88 mph cutter. His fastball sits at 91-93 mph and touches 95 with enough riding action to keep it off barrels. He'll flash an average changeup with some tumble, though it's too firm in the mid-80s and he doesn't trust it very much. While scouts praise McElvain's competitiveness and ability to pitch above his pure stuff, he'll need to throw more strikes to remain in the rotation. His delivery should be conducive to better control, and he gets into trouble when he falls behind in the count because he lacks the arsenal to overpower hitters. He'll get the chance to start in pro ball but could wind up as a slider-heavy reliever.

A seldom-used reliever at Oklahoma State, Peterson transferred to Illinois-Chicago for the 2022 season. He posted a 3.89 ERA with 102 strikeouts in 88 innings, coming within three of a Flames single-season whiff record that has stood since 1958. The little lefty pounds the strike zone with four pitches, including a low-90s fastball with good carry and an average slider and changeup.

One of the best fifth-year senior values in the 2022 Draft, Young led NCAA Division I in runs in each of the last two seasons (83 in 2021, 89 this spring) and set Louisiana Tech career records for games (249) and hits (297). He batted .364/.506/.644 with 12 homers and 28 steals in 64 contests this year while displaying bat-to-ball skills, plus speed and fine range and instincts at shortstop. His fringy arm strength likely will dictate a move to second base in pro ball.

Crampton, who was born in Taiwan and raised in Oakland, batted .296/.367/.367 while playing consistent shortstop at Stanford. His cousin is tennis player Taro Daniel, who represented Japan at the 2016 Summer Olympics in Rio de Janeiro.

After being taken in Round 38 of the 2019 Draft, Romero headed to Vanderbilt, but after not playing in the shortened 2020 season and sparingly in '21, he transferred to Miami for this spring. He’s a left-handed hitter with power (12 homers in '22) at a premium position who should be good enough to stay behind the plate. His bat will determine if he has the chance to be a regular, with a backup role more likely.

Lewis was a solid two-way player at Marina High School in Huntington Beach, Calif., before heading to join a Santa Barbara program that has produced first-round arms like Michael McGreevy last year and Dillon Tate in 2015, not to mention Shane Bieber in 2016. After redshirting in 2020, Lewis spent most of 2021 pitching capably as a starter before helping the Gauchos win the Big West title again as their all-conference Friday night starter. The 6-foot-5 Lewis brings a unique array of pitches to the mound, all coming from an athletic frame. While his fastball typically sits in the 90-92 mph range, topping out at 95, he uses excellent extension and high spin rates to get swings and misses on the pitch up in the zone. He throws a solid 11-to-5 downer of a curveball in the upper 70s that misses bats consistently. He’s fiddled with a work-in-progress changeup and mixes in an occasional cutter, though that might be his worst pitch. Lewis also throws a legitimate knuckleball that he would float up around 80 mph that confounded hitters and catchers alike. Though he didn’t throw it much, it’s the kind of pitch he could turn to if being a “regular” pitcher doesn’t work out. Generally a strike-thrower, Lewis got tired at the end of the season and his command suffered, but his combination of size, interesting stuff and strong analytics, not to mention the track record of his school, should have teams interested in seeing how it works at the next level.

A three-star tight end prospect in high school, Cumbest played two sports in his first three years at Mississippi State, catching five passes for the football team before missing last fall after surgery to repair a dislocated shoulder. Also a part-timer in his first three seasons on the diamond, he helped the Bulldogs win the College World Series last June. He has made progress while finally getting regular playing time in 2022 and has the upside to factor in the first five rounds. At 6-foot-6 and 235 pounds, Cumbest is built along the lines of Giancarlo Stanton and his strength and bat speed give him well above-average raw pop. He won't fully tap into his power potential until he gets more upright in his stance to gain more leverage with his right-handed stroke. He also needs more at-bats to continue to refine his approach at the plate and learn how to cope with breaking balls and changeups. Extremely athletic for his size, Cumbest has solid speed and good instincts on the bases. He has seen time in center field this spring but spent most of the season in left field. He has average arm strength and could become a solid defender on the corners with more experience.

Jenkins has an unusual profile as an extremely aggressive hitter who strikes out in nearly a quarter of his plate appearances yet also makes a lot of loud contact. Though he has played only first base at Georgia Tech, he may have some untapped defensive value as well. He batted third in a Yellow Jackets lineup that could have as many as seven players drafted in July. Built along the lines of Andrew Vaughn, Jenkins has a compact right-handed swing with plenty of bat speed and strength. He gets to most of his plus raw power, generating high exit velocities and driving the ball with authority to all fields. He rarely walks and is vulnerable to sliders, so there is some concern as to how his approach will play at the next level. More athletic than he looks, Jenkins can show average run times out of the batter's box and moves well for his size. An average to solid defender at first base, he has a stronger arm than most at the position. A third baseman and catcher in high school, he might merit a look at the hot corner or in left or right field.
Estep hit .302 with 13 home runs and 16 stolen bases for the Wildcats in 2022. His uncle, Chris, was selected in the 12th round by the Pirates in the 1988 Draft and played five seasons in the Minors.

JSerra Catholic High School in San Juan Capistrano, Calif., has produced a good amount of Draft talent, most notably former No. 1 overall pick Royce Lewis. After three pitchers from the program got drafted a year ago, including current Giants’ Top 30 prospect Eric Silva, this year could be Gough’s turn, especially after he went to the MLB Draft Combine and struck out all five batters he faced in game action. Gough is an aggressive right-hander with arm strength who has the chance to have at least three average or better offerings in the future. His fastball can touch 95 mph, but typically sits in the 91- to 94-mph range, thrown with good spin rates up in the zone. While he typically features a slurvy breaking ball in the upper 70s, he showed a distinct slider and curve, the latter of which led to three of his strikeouts at the Combine. He has some feel for his changeup, but it’s a work in progress. The 6-foot-2 righty is more control over command right now, but he does a solid job of finding the strike zone. He attacks and goes right after hitters, leading some to wonder what he would look like in a relief role where he could just blow it out for an inning at a time. He’s committed to Oregon State, where he’d likely get a chance to start.

Harrison pitched very well out of the Dirtbags bullpen in 2020 and '21, saving 10 games and striking out better than 11 per nine in the latter year. His '22 wasn't as consistent, but he did show off a 90-95 mph fastball that has some darting action to it, as well as a low-80s split change and a slider thrown around 78-83 mph. He'll need to find more consistency with his secondary stuff, but scouts love the way he competes out of the bullpen.

Considering that future big league third baseman Dallas McPherson was drafted out of Randleman (N.C.) HS as a pitcher, Brannon may be the best position prospect ever produced by the Tigers. Part of 2-A state championship teams in 2021 and 2022, he led national high school players with 20 homers and 91 RBI in 34 games this spring. His offensive potential stacks up nicely against this year's prep catchers, but there are questions as to whether he can stay behind the plate. Strong and physical, Brannon generates plus raw power. He has a quick right-handed stroke and routinely produces impressive exit velocities. While he shows a willingness to use the entire field, he may have to tone down his aggressive approach against more advanced pitching. Brannon's best defensive tool is his plus arm, which can record pop times around 1.9 seconds. The North Carolina recruit has a strong work ethic but may not be more than an adequate receiver and needs to improve his framing and blocking. If he has to change positions, third base and left field would be his best-case scenarios.

Turner played his first four collegiate seasons at Kent State before transferring to Arkansas, where he slashed .323/.388/.502 in 2022. His father, Brian, was drafted in the 40th round by the Yankees in 1989.

school_name: State College of Florida Manatee - Sarasota

blurb: A swingman on a North Carolina State club that finished third at the 2021 College World Series, Villaman provided three innings of clutch relief in the super-regional finale against Arkansas to help send the Wolfpack to Omaha. He pitched for the U.S. collegiate national team last summer and has operated exclusively out of the bullpen this spring. He increased his Draft stock with a strong performance at the Atlantic Coast Conference tournament, including five shutout innings with eight strikeouts against North Carolina in a championship-game loss. Villaman relies heavily on a 90-93 mph fastball that tops out at 95, using it more than 80 percent of the time overall and more than 90 percent against left-handers, and it plays better than its velocity. He can command it all over the strike zone, with good carry at the top and nice downhill angle at the knees. He keeps right-handers off his heater by mixing in an improved low-80s changeup with fade and sink. Villaman has a strong 6-foot-2 frame and pounds the strike zone, and some scouts wonder whether he could succeed as a starter in pro ball. The biggest question is his lack of feel for spinning the ball and he doesn’t show much faith in a flat, slow slider in the upper 70s. It’s more likely that he’ll remain in a multi-inning relief role at the next level.

After a year at Division II Assumption College in Massachusetts that saw him strike out 16.3 per nine innings, Choate helped himself even more by showing well in the New England Collegiate Baseball League this summer. Being 6-foot-8 and left-handed doesn't hurt, either. Choate is up to 93 mph with his fastball and leans on it heavily. He does have a solid upper-70s slider and 76-77 mph changeup, registering super-high whiff rates on his secondary stuff. Command (5 BB/9 in college) might hurt him, but his strike-throwing has improved over the years.
Agnos hit .330 with 56 runs scored, while also posting a 2.31 ERA on the mound in 23 1/3 innings for the Pirates in 2022. His brother, Jake, was drafted in the fourth round by the Yankees in ‘19.

The Langley Blaze club team in British Columbia, Canada, has produced some serious talent, including big leaguers like Brett Lawrie and Tyler O’Neill. This year could provide another top Draft talent in Zibin, a projectable right-hander who reclassified from 2023 to be eligible for this year’s Draft and then spent a lot of time at TXNL Academy in Florida to raise his profile. Zibin has an intriguing combination of size (at 6-foot-3) and stuff. His fastball has been up to 95-96 mph at times this spring, sitting mostly around 93 mph. His best secondary pitch is actually his changeup, thrown with good sink and good arm speed -- a pitch he’s willing to throw in any count. His breaking ball, a 77-80 mph slurvy slider, can be effective, but it’s much less consistent. Zibin tends to be around the plate, though like with most young pitchers, he’ll need to refine his overall command. Because he reclassified, he’ll be young for his class, just 17 years old, something that will appeal to many teams who run things like age through their models. As a Canadian prepster, he can return to the high school ranks there for what amounts to 13th grade and be eligible again next year, and he’s committed to South Carolina in 2023.

A native of Alaska, Jessee closed some games for Gonzaga in 2021 and was again a big contributor out of the Zags bullpen this spring. He has the kind of power repertoire that can work late in games, with a fastball that has reached triple digits in the past and was more in the 93-98 mph range in '22. His low-80s slider has the makings of a solid secondary offering and he’s shown a little feel for a changeup. While he has plenty of arm strength, he’s been hit more than expected because he is much more control-over-command and gets too much of the plate.

Strong and athletic, Showalter has plenty of arm strength on the mound. The right-hander is up to 95 mph with his fastball, sitting in the low 90s, and has the chance to have a solid average slider as well. The South Florida recruit does have some effort and stiffness in his delivery, leading some scouts to think there’s some reliever risk here.

Sandlin was part of an all-prospect rotation in 2021 at Eastern Oklahoma State JC, joining Christian McGowan (who signed with the Phillies as a seventh-rounder) and Andrew Walling (who pitched three innings this year at Mississippi State after transferring). Sandlin moved on to Oklahoma, where he had an up-and-down regular season before coming up big repeatedly in the postseason. He struck out 10 in a win over Kansas State in the Big 12 Conference tournament, beat Florida in the NCAA regionals and fanned 12 in a College World Series victory over Texas A&M. Sandlin's best pitch is a 78-80 mph curveball with depth and horizontal action that flashes plus, though he doesn't use it as often as his solid 82-85 mph slider with more lateral movement. The key for him at the next level will be missing more bats with his fastball, which sits at 91-93 mph and tops out at 96 with a bit of armside run. He'll also have to refine a mid-80s changeup that needs more velocity separation from his heater and more fade to be effective. Though Sandlin throws strikes, his control isn't as advanced as his command. He'll need to add more power or life to his fastball, which gets pounded when he leaves it in the middle of the zone. If he can't make the necessary adjustments, he could become a breaking ball-heavy reliever.

Skipper made 21 appearances and one start for the Tigers this year, one season after he was named to the NCBWA Stopper of the Year Watch List. His great-grandfather, William Henry Cuppett played in the Minors in the 1930s, and his father and grandfather were both college athletes.

Don Bosco Prep in New Jersey has had 11 players drafted in school history, but none have made it to the big leagues and no one from the high school program has been selected since 2012. Dana, a big, strong, hard-throwing right-hander, hopes to change that after throwing well at summer showcase events like Perfect Game National and matching up against top high school pitching prospect Dylan Lesko at this year’s National High School Invitational. The two main things that stand out about Dana are his size and his arm strength. The 6-foot-5 right-hander reminds some of Noah Syndergaard on the mound, both in terms of his physicality and his flowing locks. Dana has been up to 95 mph consistently this spring and touched higher than that in shorter stints last summer, showing the ability to hold his velocity deep into outings. His fastball can straighten out at times, and his ability to land his curve -- a hard downer thrown around 75-76 mph that flashes plus -- for strikes more consistently will keep hitters from cheating on the fastball. He does have a changeup, but it will need work at the next level. Dana tends to command his fastball pretty well and when he’s on top and out front with his breaking ball, it’s a real bat-misser, but like with a lot of young power pitchers, his command will need refinement. It’s easy to dream on more in the tank velocity-wise from this Kentucky recruit, the kind of projection that could have him taken in the first several rounds of the Draft.

After starting his college career at Patrick Henry Community College in Virginia in 2021, Ellerts moved on to Florida Southwestern and his perfect 10-0 season earned him an NJCAA All-American honorable mention before he went on to pitch well in the Cape Cod League leading up to the Draft. He's a big, strong right-hander with a fastball in the 90-94 mph range and a slider that could be average in the future. While he has a decent feel for pitching, the effort in his delivery gives him more of a reliever look. He's committed to go to the University of Florida if the Draft doesn't work out.

Boyd is the latest speedy Mississippi high school outfielder in the tradition of Billy Hamilton and 2019 White Sox fourth-rounder James Beard. The track record of Magnolia State prep position players is scary -- Austin Riley is a rare and recent exception -- and he didn’t help his cause by trying to do too much at the plate this spring. Yet his quickness and signability as a Northwest Mississippi CC commit still could land him in the first five rounds. Boyd can flash top-of-the-scale speed and get from the right side of the plate to first base in less than four seconds at times. He has the wheels to become a dynamic basestealer and to cover plenty of ground in center field, though he needs to refine his defensive instincts. He compensates for fringy arm strength by getting to balls quickly. On the showcase circuit last summer, Boyd impressed scouts with his quick, compact right-handed swing and his pitch recognition, displaying the ability to make quality contact against high velocity with wood bats. He got too power-conscious at times as a senior, and that’s definitely not his game though he does have bat speed and some projectable strength. He needs to get back to concentrating on hitting line drives and getting on base to take advantage of his speed.

It’s possible that if Brutti were from, say, California, and not Texas, he’d have gotten more attention this spring. He really jumped on the map with breakout performance this past winter at Prep Baseball Report’s Super 60 event in Chicago, where he showed a jump in velocity. That held all spring, as the 6-foot-3 right-hander was up to 97 mph with his sinking fastball consistently, holding his velocity. He complements his plus heater with an 87-mph slider that also flashes plus. While he has some feel for a changeup, he doesn’t throw it often. Skeptics see some reliever risk due to a modified stretch delivery and some effort in his delivery, but scouts who like him counter that he repeats the delivery well and fills up the strike zone. He’ll head to South Florida if the Draft doesn’t work out.

Oppor is the best mound prospect to come out of the Wisconsin high school ranks since Connor Prielipp three years ago, though he's not in the same class as the fellow lefty who's a potential first-rounder after attending college at Alabama. He should become the highest Badger State prep pitcher taken since Mariners third-rounder Tony Butler in 2006. He's athletic, already can push his fastball to 96 mph and is very signable as a Gulf Coast State (Fla.) JC commit. While Oppor is still a work in progress, he sits around 90-92 mph with a fastball that plays up with the sink and run he creates from a low three-quarters slot. His best secondary offering is a sweeping upper-70s slider, though he doesn't trust it as much as his mid-70s curveball with more depth. He has had little use for a changeup to this point and has yet to show much feel for the pitch. The club that invests in Oppor will buy into his athleticism and his projection. Though he stands just 6-foot-1, he has room to add at least 20 pounds and create extension that adds to his deception. He needs more strength and consistency with his stuff and control and will require time to develop, but he's intriguing as a high school arm with upside who'll sign for a low-six-figure bonus.

A mainstay on U.S. national teams since he was 12, Clifford won gold medals at international competitions with the 12-and-under squad in 2015 and the 15-and-under club in 2018. He has the tools to be one of the better all-around hitters in the 2022 high school class, though his stock took a slight hit when he didn't light up the showcase circuit last summer. If he doesn't go early enough in the Draft to sign, he'll head to Vanderbilt and be eligible again as a sophomore in 2024. With a picturesque left-handed swing and a good bat path, Clifford is equipped to hit for average and power. He has an advanced approach at the plate, focusing on driving balls from gap to gap while rarely chasing pitches out of the strike zone. With his bat speed and the strength in his 6-foot-3 frame, he could provide 20 or more homers per year once he starts driving the ball in the air more regularly. Scouts compare Clifford to Seth Beer with more athleticism, but unlike Beer he has a realistic chance to play the outfield. Though he has fringy speed and his reads and routes must improve, he's committed to working on his quickness and defense. He could stay in right field with solid arm strength and could at least be an average defender at first base, where he has played with Team USA.

Nevin, the brother of Orioles third baseman Tyler Nevin and son of Angels interim manager Phil Nevin, batted .304 for the Bears this year. In addition to his regular position of left field, he’s demonstrated an ability to play first base.

Every year there are prospects who “pop up” onto Draft radars, players who were relatively unknown before the spring but take a huge step forward in their Draft year to become more seriously considered in the early rounds. Witherspoon, whose twin brother Kyson is also a high school senior, has become one of the more intriguing ones in Florida, coming out of the Jacksonville-area high school ranks. Teams have been scrambling to see Witherspoon this spring as his velocity started to climb. Those in the area who have known him always knew of his ability to spin a breaking ball and that hasn’t changed, with a plus curveball that has registered spin rates up around 3,000 rpm. Strong and athletic at 6-foot-3, he has a stronger lower half might be the reason why his fastball has ticked upwards and made him a much more interesting prospect. He’s now up to 95 mph and sitting around 92-93 mph with the pitch, also thrown with high spin rates. He’s also shown a surprising feel for his changeup at times. Witherspoon is still a bit raw and more about projection, though he tends to be around the strike zone. He doesn’t have the cleanest delivery and some see a little reliever risk, but the Jacksonville recruit has a huge up arrow next to his name.

After starring at Ironwood Ridge High School outside of Tucson, Ariz., Baez traveled north about 90 minutes to play for Arizona State. He made an immediate contribution to the Sun Devils as a super-utility type in the pandemic and eventually became their main catcher as a sophomore in 2021, earning all Pac-12 honorable mention status in the process. He made the team outright in 2022, again as the primary backstop, finishing the season with a .965 OPS. While none of Baez’s tools jump off the page, he definitely performed in a premium conference, upping his numbers to a .355/.424/.664 line with eight of his 10 homers coming against Pac-12 opponents. He has a decent approach at the plate with an ability to work counts and draw some walks while limiting his swing and miss. He continued to tap into his power more, with it mostly showing up to the pull side, and he didn’t have any issues turning around premium velocity. Baez has shown some positional versatility, seeing a good amount of time at second base while also playing first, third and left field. His value is highest if he can stick behind the plate, with the chance to be an average receiver, something any team taking him in this year’s Draft is sure to give him every opportunity to do.

The 6-foot-3 lefty didn't put up great numbers at Portland (4.75 career ERA), but he still missed bats, raising his strikeout rate every spring. Teams interested will almost certainly view him as a lefty reliever, one with a funky crossfire delivery that fires fastballs up to 94 mph and an above-average changeup.

Janas took the Big Ten by storm in 2021, as he won the regular-season batting crown (.391). As a freshman, Janis was selected to the All-Big Ten First Team, and followed that up by being selected to the All-Big Ten Second Team in ‘22. His cousin, Mark Payton, was drafted in ‘14 by the Yankees in the seventh round. Mark made his MLB debut with the Reds in ‘20.

Curialle took over at shortstop at JSerra Catholic High School from 2017 No. 1 overall pick Royce Lewis and was a late-round pick in 2019 with teams knowing he had a strong commitment to UCLA. A .325/.357/.525 slash line in the pandemic-shortened 2020 season set a pretty high bar, and he’s struggled to meet it since. He started the 2022 season extremely slowly and lost his shortstop job to freshman Cody Schrier, but righted the ship and finished well (.319/.395/.479). Scouts aren’t exactly sure what to make of Curialle as he hasn’t quite developed into the offensive threat they thought he might when he first arrived on campus. He does have the ability to make hard contact from the right side of the plate with some pull side power to tap into, but swing-and-miss tendencies have kept him from getting to it consistently. An average runner who is fairly athletic, it’s unclear where Curialle will play defensively at the next level. He has the arm strength for third, and he might not have the defensive actions or mobility to stay there. He played a good amount of right field this spring, fairly uncomfortably, but left field might be an option. Finishing strongly in the Pac-12 certainly won’t hurt him, and perhaps he can be an offensive-minded utility type who sees time at third, first and a corner outfield spot at the next level.

Baldwin has been a staple for UNC Wilmington over the past several seasons, as he has been selected to the First Team All-CAA twice and the Second Team All-CAA once. Baldwin’s dad, Chuck, was the 1986 ACC Player of the Year and was drafted by Cleveland in 1987.

A 31st-round pick by the Mariners out of a Texas high school in 2019, Meador spent his first two college seasons at Texas Christian before transferring to Dallas Baptist and striking out 91 with a 5.24 ERA in 68 2/3 innings this spring. His primary weapon is a low-90s fastball that tops out at 96 mph, with his low arm slot producing a flat approach angle and good carry. His mid-80s changeup has some fade and more promise than his upper-70s curveball. He projects as a reliever at the next level.

Vogel spent two years at Eastern Florida State before moving on to become Jacksonville's closer in 2022. The six-footer finished with 10 saves then pitched well in relief in the Cape Cod League and actually has four pitches, with a 91-95 mph fastball and a curve, slider and changeup that all can work, though his delivery and command both say he'll continue to be a reliever only.

Marcum is the nephew of former MLB player Shaun Marcum. Shaun was a third-round pick by the Blue Jays in the 2003 draft and spent five of his nine MLB seasons north of the border. Marcum has verbally committed to the University of Tennessee.

A favorite of area scouts, Fulgencio began his college athletic career as an Oklahoma State running back. He transferred to Cowley County (Kan.) CC and played in the outfield during the shortened 2020 season, then taught himself to pitch and refined his delivery while watching YouTube videos during the pandemic layoff. A pitcher the last two years, he's intriguing as a lefty reliever with a 90-96 mph fastball and tight slider. He went 7-0 with five saves, a 1.75 ERA and a 60/8 K/BB ratio in 46 1/3 innings this spring as the Tigers reached the finals of the Junior College World Series. He'll transfer to Oklahoma if he doesn't turn pro, though at age 23 he should be very signable.

There’s a familiar name for baseball fans. The son of two-time MLB All-Star and four-time Gold Glove-winning center fielder Marquis Grissom, Grissom Jr. struck out 57 over 61 frames for Georgia Tech this year.

Rujano’s size and arm strength certainly made him stand out on last summer’s showcase circuit, and he made the rounds, pitching at events like Perfect Game’s National Showcase and the East Coast Professional Showcase. While he hasn’t made any huge steps forward in terms of pitchability, the raw tools on the mound still stand out. The big right-hander checks off all the boxes in terms of his physicality, using his 6-foot-4 frame well and making it easy to dream on more to come once he gets pro instruction. He’s been up to 96 mph with his fastball, typically sitting in the low 90s now. He has a tight slider but struggles to spin his breaking ball consistently, and while there’s some feel for a changeup, it’s also inconsistent. Rujano uses a methodical delivery with a big kick out of the windup and is still much more thrower than pitcher, with a need to work on his command. The South Florida recruit will be 19 at Draft time, and any team interested in selecting him will likely have to be patient on the development side for him to tap into his raw tools consistently on the mound.

One of the fastest prospects in the 2020 high school class, Johnson went unselected in the five-round Draft and headed to Oklahoma State. He got just 13 at-bats with the Cowboys as a freshman, then led the Cape Cod League with 14 steals last summer and transferred to Kansas State. He prospered with the Wildcats, batting .345/.419/.593 with 12 homers and as many swipes. Johnson's plus-plus speed remains his best attribute but scouts question his instincts and ability to make the most of it. He has the quickness to be a prolific basestealer but doesn't get good reads or jumps. The same is true with his defense, as he's a shaky outfielder with a below-average arm, and Kansas State chose to deploy him in left field rather than in center despite his wheels. Johnson made some progress with his right-handed stroke this spring and showed the ability to impact the ball more than he had in the past. He has 15-homer potential but his pop also can get him into trouble, as he'll focus on launching balls to his pull side rather than getting on base and taking advantage of his speed. He'll need to adopt a more patient approach and do a better job of using the entire field to hit pro pitching.

The Florida Junior College Player of the Year, Flint spent 2019 and '20 at Maryland before transferring to Chipola, where he finished with a .370/.496/.708 slash line. At 21, he’s a little bit older than a typical JUCO product, but he has offensive upside as a hitter with power from the left side, though some scouts worry about the lack of bat speed. He’s not unathletic but is likely destined to play first or left field because of a fringy arm.

Dylan Ross, RHP, Georgia

Ross underwent season-ending surgery in March but has a decent track record–he transferred to Georgia after one season as an ace at Northwest Florida State College and a strong freshman year for Eastern Kentucky. Ross is the grandson of John Small, who played five seasons in the NFL for the Falcons and Lions, and the brother of University of Tampa pitcher Dalton Ross.

After spending his freshman year largely as a reliever, the 6-foot-7 Pfennigs has spent the past three as a part of the Beavers’ rotation, with mixed results. He has a low-90s fastball that tops out at 95 mph, an upper-70s curve and harder, low-80s slider. He hasn’t missed a ton of bats (7.3 K/9) in his career, and because he gets too much of the plate, he’s been more hittable than he should be, with some area scouts seeing no real gains stuff-wise since his freshman year. Still, his size and solid breaking stuff could make him an intriguing senior sign.

The right-hander made his way from Quebec down to Chipola Junior College and finished the 2022 season with a 2.80 ERA and an 11.33 K/9 rate. He was typically in the 90-95 mph range and touched 96 during his time in the Draft League and can show off a solid slider and even some feel for a changeup. He has more of a reliever profile at the next level.

Wisconsin continues to produce quality infield prospects, with Kilen the latest in a line that includes 2016 first-rounder Gavin Lux, 2018 third-rounder Owen Miller and 2021 supplemental first-rounder Noah Miller. He's part of the same Hitters Baseball program that has sent Lux, Owen Miller, Jarred Kelenic and Ben Rortvedt to the big leagues, and several other players into the top five rounds of the Draft. Kilen led Milton HS to its first-ever Division 1 state title this spring, scoring the winning run in the championship game. One of the best contact hitters among 2022 prepsters, Kilen didn't strike out a single time at the Area Code Games last August. He has outstanding hand-eye coordination, feel for the barrel and a quick left-handed stroke built for hitting line drives, though scouts would like to see him add some muscle and drive the ball more consistently. He projects to hit 12-15 homers per season but presently hits too many ground balls. Kilen's speed and arm strength are more average than plus, so there are some questions about his long-term future at shortstop. His proponents believe the Louisville recruit has the actions, hands and instincts to remain at short. Others think he's destined to become an offensive-minded second baseman.

In 2019, Newell was a talented high school outfielder from Pennsylvania who landed on MLB Pipeline’s Top 100 based on his raw tools, though they didn’t consistently show up in games. He didn’t get selected until close to the end of the Draft and honored his commitment to the University of Virginia. After opening some eyes with a strong showing in the shortened 2020 season, he’s looked more like the raw player he was in high school in 2021 and 2022. A left-handed hitter, Newell does show glimpses of being able to drive the ball to all fields. He needs to refine his approach, as there has been way too much swing-and-miss in his game. He has cut his strikeout rate and drawn more walks this season, which has helped him get to his power more. He punishes fastballs but struggles against breaking stuff. Newell runs well and can steal a base, and he’s reached double digits in home runs and stolen bases this spring. Newell’s speed should allow him to stick in center field, which certainly doesn’t hurt his profile. It all comes down to if he can keep improving his approach and make more contact. There are a lot of tools to unlock here, and while Newell isn’t the Top 100 prospect he was as a prepster, there are bound to be teams who will be willing to roll the dice on his skillset.

Gatorade's 2018 Rhode Island High School Player of the Year, Sears stayed home to play at CC of Rhode Island and helped the Knights to a fifth-place finish at the Division III Junior College World Series as a freshman. He transferred to Houston and got knocked around as a starter in 2021 before starring as a reliever this spring, ranking 10th in NCAA Division I in both saves (10) and walks per nine innings (1.1). He doesn't miss a lot of bats but induces a lot of groundouts with a 91-96 mph sinker, flashes a solid slider and keeps left-handers at bay with a lively changeup. His size and low arm slot create difficult angle for right-handers.

While Bow both started and relieved at College of Southern Nevada, the 6-foot-4 right-hander was named his junior college region’s Reliever of the Year after picking up five saves and striking out 13.8 per nine before continuing to miss bats in the Cape Cod League. The 6-foot-4 right-hander was up to 94-95 mph with his fastball in 2022 with good spin rates and complemented it with a low-80s slider, which can also generate swings and misses. Command issues (4.97 BB/9) point to a future in the bullpen.

The Venezuelan-born Izarra played his high school ball at Miami Christian in Florida and then stayed home to start his college career at the University of Miami. After picking up just 15 at-bats as a freshman in 2021, the shortstop transferred to a junior college, Florida Southwestern State. While he put up solid numbers (.341/.427/.609) in 2022, scouts worry about his light bat. He can definitely pay shortstop long-term, with great hands and a plus arm, so a team interested in taking him will be buying the glove and hoping that added strength leads to more offensive upside.

One of the best two-way talents in the 2019 Draft, Osmond was the best pitching prospect to come out of Jenks (Okla.) HS since Josh Johnson and also a legitimate shortstop with raw offensive potential. A potential second-rounder who plummeted to the Nationals in the 35th round because of signability, he has enjoyed only sporadic success as a full-time pitcher at Oklahoma State. He pitched better than in April and his stock was climbing toward the third round, but then he regressed in May. Osmond sits in the low 90s and can reach 96 mph with his fastball, but it gets hit hard because it features only modest armside run and he doesn't command it well. His low-80s slider is clearly his best offering, flashing as plus with horizontal and vertical action. He also can snap off some quality curveballs in the upper 70s, but he doesn't use his bender or his fading low-80s changeup very often. Working out of a high three-quarters slot with a quick arm, Osmond often battles his control and command. He's still a potential No. 4 starter if he can improve his fastball quality, work his curveball and changeup in more often and do a better job of locating his pitches. But it's looking increasingly likely that he's destined to become a fastball/slider reliever.
Jaha’s father, John, had a 10-year Major League career with the Brewers and A’s and was named an All-Star in 1999. Jaha is currently committed to Oregon.

Tulloch has taken a circuitous route to this point, starting at Wingate University in 2020, then moving to West Virginia the following year, when he was taken in Round 17 by the Dodgers. From there, he headed to Arzona State, where the left-hander showed off a fastball up to 94 mph to go along with a low-80s slder and changeup, both of which helped him strike out 11.4 per nine. He also walked 5.1 per nine and the control issues followed him to the Cape Cod League this summer, but scouts feel his stuff and command will pay better in shorter stints out of the bullpen.

O’Tremba enjoyed a breakout season in 2022, when he hit .351 with 11 home runs and 56 RBIs for the Wildcats, earning him Pac-12 All-Conference honors. O’Tremba’s cousin, Anthony Bemboom, was a 22nd-round pick by the Los Angeles Angels in ‘12; Bemboom currently plays in the Orioles’ farm system.

Roberts spent two years at Tennessee Tech before hitting over .300 in the Cape Cod League and transferring to Florida State. This spring with the Seminoles, he hit .300/.373/.461 with a knack for hard contact and driving the ball the other way. He has the chance to hit at the next level and has shown an ability to defend well wherever he’s placed, with the chance to stay on the dirt.

Since heading to BYU in 2020, McKeehan has done nothing but pitch in relief. He’s always missed bats (13.9 K/9 in his career), but it wasn’t until '22 that he started having more consistent success, finishing with a 1.57 ERA and whiffing 16 per nine using a low-90s fastball that topped out at 93 mph and a power downer breaking ball, thrown around 80 mph, that missed a ton of bats. McKeehan got a lot of hitters to chase with that pitch, and he will have to improve his command (5.5 BB/9) at the next level, but he did help himself by throwing well in the Cape Cod League before the Draft.
Becker started his college career at Vanderbilt before transferring to Texas Tech. His father, Kevin, was drafted in the 34th round by the Red Sox in 1991 and played three seasons in the Minors.

Dana played his first four collegiate seasons at Seton Hall before he transferred to Connecticut. With the Huskies, Dana hit .313 with 21 doubles and 12 home runs in 2022. Dana has two ball players for brothers: Caden is the No. 119 MLB Draft prospect in the ‘22 class per MLB Pipeline, while Cullen was drafted in the 30th round by the Padres in ‘18.

Karros followed in the footsteps of his father -- 1992 Rookie of the Year and longtime Dodgers first baseman Eric Karros -- by suiting up for the Bruins. The righty was UCLA’s opening day starter in 2021 but suffered a season-ending back injury that also kept him out of action in '22.

The defensive glue on the U.S. national team that won a gold medal at the 2017 18-and-under World Cup and featured 11 future first-rounders, Young struggled offensively on the showcase circuit the following summer and went undrafted as a Washington high schooler in 2019. He hit 16 homers in his first full season at Vanderbilt but also led NCAA Division I with 84 strikeouts in 61 games, with some scouts wondering if a left shoulder injury that required offseason surgery contributed to his whiffs. That doesn't appear to be the case because he has continued to swing for the fences and pile up strikeouts while batting .207/.327/.383 this spring. Young has transformed himself since high school, adding 20 pounds and getting much more uphill with his stroke from both sides of the plate. After showing little power as a prepster, he now has average pop that plays to his pull side as a right-handed hitter and to all fields as lefty. To realize his 20-homer potential in pro ball, he'll need to tone down his stroke and stop chasing pitches out of the strike zone. Young plays quicker than his average speed thanks to his advanced instincts on the bases and in the field. He covers ground at shortstop, where his hands, strong arm and internal clock make him a solid and reliable defender. If he doesn't hit enough to be a regular, he has the versatility and skills to fill a utility role.

Dorsey earned FCSAA Second-Team All-State honors after striking out 15.65 per nine innings in his first year at Gulf Coast State, then went on to pitch very well for the Savannah Bananas this summer leading up to the Draft. While his fastball sat 89 mph and touched 92 mph, it was sneaky quick and elicited swings and misses. Tall and lanky, the left-hander may fill out and add more velocity along with sharper secondary offerings, with his breaking ball still developing. He's just 19 but could eventually turn into a back-of-the-rotation type.

A 12th-round pick by the Pirates last year after starring as a two-way player at Orange Coast (Calif.) JC, Martinez opted to transfer to Oklahoma. Used primarily as a pitcher by the Sooners, he struck out 14 against Northwestern State in his second start but regressed over the course of the spring and finished the season in the bullpen. When he was going good, Martinez reached 95 mph with impressive carry on his fastball, showed some feel for a curveball with depth and mixed in a decent changeup. He also offers some left-handed power potential, though his future is definitely on the mound.

home_city: West Toronto (High Park / The Junction South)

blurb: In 2020, Sikorski became the first Western Michigan pitcher to earn a win over a Pac-12 school since 1989. Sikorski's father, Brian, was drafted in the fourth round by the Astros in 1995. Brian pitched two seasons for the Rangers, Padres and Indians, and now works as a pro scout with the Marlins.

Neal was standing out as an underclassman at IMG Academy in Florida in 2021 when he reclassified to be a part of the 2022 Draft class. He’s now one of the top high school catchers with solid tools both at and behind the plate that were on display at summer showcase events like Major League Baseball’s PDP League, and he was the youngest player at the Perfect Game All-American Classic. He’s continued to look like a top three-round type of catching prospect during his senior season. Neal is a young backstop with the ability to do a lot of things well on both sides of the ball. He has a nice left-handed stroke with an advanced approach at the plate. He uses all fields well and he’s coming into some more power, especially to the pull side. With a prototypical stocky catcher’s body, Neal has a slightly above-average arm that’s accurate and quick. He does a very good job receiving and has shown an ability to catch a high-level pitching staff at IMG. Committed to LSU, he reminds some of Tucker Barnhart in terms of being a solid all-around backstop without a plus tool.

The Virginia high school ranks have been pretty fertile ground for Draft talent over the years, though the true stars have largely come in the batter’s box, with the likes of David Wright and Justin Upton. There hasn’t been a prep pitcher taken in the top five rounds since 2016, when the Orioles nabbed Brenan Hanifee in the fourth round. Huesman put himself in position to get off that schneid after a strong summer on the showcase circuit that was punctuated with an 18-strikeout exclamation point in Perfect Game’s WWBA World Championship last fall. The lefty has solid arm strength with a fastball that he can run up to 95 mph but usually settles into the 90-92 mph range, a pitch with solid spin rates that features good running life. He backs it up with a very good slider that averages around 76 mph with high spin. It can flash plus at times, though it also can get slurvy as his outings go on. His changeup kills spin and has some fade to it, though it’s behind the other two pitches. Huesman is athletic on the mound and repeats his delivery fairly well, which leads to a decent amount of strikes, though there is some concern about effort in his mechanics and the ability to maintain his stuff, leading some to think there’s reliever risk here. A team taking the Coastal Carolina recruit in the top five rounds will think the southpaw has every chance to be a member of a big league rotation.

Walters wasn’t heavily recruited coming out of high school in Florida and headed to Eastern Florida State College, a two-year school in Brevard County. After a year there shortened by the pandemic, he moved to Miami and made an immediate impact out of the bullpen. He became the Canes’ closer in 2022 and has been among the Division I leaders in saves. At this point, Walters is pretty much a one-pitch pitcher. The 6-foot-4 right-hander throws his fastball more than 90 percent of the time and goes right after hitters with it. It tops out at 99 mph and averages over 95. Even though opponents know it’s coming, they can’t hit it, with a whiff rate of over 40 percent this spring. There’s deception in his delivery, as he steps across his body and hides the ball well, making it tough to pick up, and the pitch has really good life up in the zone. Walters does have a slurvy breaking ball at present, but it’s a below-average show-me pitch at this point. He’s been filling up the strike zone consistently this year, and while he obviously will have to work on finding a secondary pitch to his unhittable fastball, it’s hard to argue with the success he’s had at the end of Miami’s pen.

There have been a grand total of eight high school position players from Hawaii to be taken in the top 10 rounds of the Draft since the turn of the century, with just one so far, Isiah Kiner-Falefa, to post a positive WAR. Arquette, an infielder like Kiner-Falefa, hopes to be the next after impressing scouts at events like the Area Code Games last summer and Perfect Game’s WWBA World Championship in the fall. An excellent athlete who is also a basketball standout, Arquette has added nearly nearly 25 pounds of muscle to his 6-foot-4 frame since his sophomore year of high school. He’s starting to figure things out at the plate and has the chance to hit from the right side of the plate, with plenty of raw power to tap into as he continues to physically mature. Arquette has solid defensive actions, with good hands and a strong arm. Because of his size and lack of pure foot speed, most see a move to third in his future, with a chance to grow into an offensive profile that fits at the hot corner. A team that believes in the Washington recruit’s offensive upside could take him at some point on Day 2 and give him the chance to stick at short and live up to the lofty comparisons he gets locally to another tall shortstop who loved to play basketball: Cal Ripken Jr.

Wimmer hit .312 in 2022 and was the only player for the Gamecocks to start every game this season. His father, Chris, was drafted in the eighth round by the Giants in 1992 and played on the ‘92 U.S. Olympic team. Chris now works as a scout with the Tigers.

Much of the attention regarding high school bats in the Las Vegas area is centered around Carl Crawford’s son, Justin. But scouts would be remiss if they passed up Neville, another talented toolsy outfielder from the area who could give Basic High School its first draftee since right-hander Andrew Church was a second-round pick back in 2013. Any evaluation of Neville centers around his premium athleticism. The left-handed center fielder reminds some of a young Cody Bellinger. He’s an easily plus runner who should be able to steal a base and cover a lot of ground in the outfield. At the plate, there’s some whip to his swing, which should generate power, especially as he adds strength to his 6-foot-3 frame, but there is some swing-and-miss to his game that concerns some scouts. Most feel Neville, an Arkansas recruit, will be able to play center field for a long time and, as a former pitcher, has a plus arm from the outfield as well. Scouts have been watching his ability to make contact closely this spring, with he and Crawford possibly giving the Vegas high school scene a pair of early-round prospects for the second year in a row after Tyler Whitaker and Tanner McDougal went in the top five rounds in 2021.

Reilly played his high school ball for former big leaguer Jack Wilson at Thousand Oaks High School in California before beginning his college career at Hawaii. He transferred to Grand Canyon following the pandemic-shortened 2020 season, pitched briefly in '21 before establishing himself as the Lopes’ closer this season. He set a school record with 14 saves, and the 5-foot-10 right-hander used a fastball that topped out at 96 mph and averaged close to 94 mph to get it done. He backed up his heater with a mid-80s slider and, less frequently, but effectively, a changeup also thrown around 85 mph.

More than just a curiosity, Cijntje has serious ability on the mound with both arms. He throws harder right-handed, up to 96 mph with his fastball and a high-spin breaking ball that tops out at 80 mph. Lefty, he’s around 90-91 mph with a mid-70s breaker. The Mississippi State recruit impressed at the Draft Combine, where he struck out five of the six batters he faced.

Cienega High School has had one player eventually make it to the big leagues in Dodgers right-hander Andre Jackson, who was drafted as a prepster but didn’t enter pro ball until after college. Now it’s his younger brother Isaiah’s turn, though he’s making noise as a power-hitting outfielder, not a pitcher. Any team interested in Jackson will be betting on his offensive upside and projection. He has tremendous raw power from the left side of the plate, with more than enough bat speed to dream that he’ll get to that power eventually at the next level, even if his approach is a little raw. He’s athletic for his size at 6-foot-3 and is an average runner. As he fills out, he almost certainly will belong in an outfield corner, with the potential to have an average-or-better arm that would work in right field and the offensive profile to boot. He’s young for the class -- he turned 18 in May -- which will look good in some team’s models. A team interested in signing him away from his Arizona State commitment might have to be patient on the development end, but the end result could be an athletic, run-producing corner outfielder.

Hamilton High School in Chandler, Ariz., has built a strong reputation as a baseball powerhouse in the state and has produced a fair amount of Draft talent, most notably Cody Bellinger of the Dodgers. Turley -- who showed off considerable raw tools at summer events like Perfect Game National, MLB’s PDP League and the Area Code Games -- has the chance to join that group. There are some scouts who feel Turley’s combination of tools are similar to those of Elijah Green, one of the top prospects in the class, but they don’t always show up as consistently in games. He has huge raw power and is capable of hitting the ball very hard, and out, though there are swing-and-miss concerns as he is too much of a free swinger who’ll need to refine his approach at the plate. Turley does have near-elite speed, which should eventually help him be a threat on the basepaths and make him an asset in the outfield. Right now, scouts see raw instincts in terms of reads and routes, something that can be remedied with more reps. Teams interested in the Oregon State recruit might have to be patient, but his strength and speed combination will surely be enticing.

Zack Cozart and Drew Pomeranz became early-round picks after going from Collierville (Tenn.) HS to Mississippi, and Saunier may be headed on the same path. The top high school prospect in Tennessee, he got a late start on the mound this season because of a lat injury and eased back into action on tight pitch counts in April. Scouts love his projectability and polish, though he may not have enough present stuff to pay him enough to lure him away from the Rebels. Saunier operates at 88-90 mph and maxes out at 94 with armside run on his fastball, which should gain more velocity as he fills out his athletic 6-foot-4 frame. He shows aptitude for spinning the ball, with both his upper-70s slider and mid-70s curveball grading as solid and showing the potential to perhaps become plus offerings with more power. He has a lot of confidence in a fading upper-70s changeup as well. Saunier has a loose arm, works without effort and repeats his delivery well. Once he gets stronger, he could have four solid pitches with control to match. If he doesn't show an increase in velocity this spring, he'll likely wind up in college.

Major League players who are 6-foot-7 don’t come around very often, even if Aaron Judge and Oneil Cruz are very impressive current exceptions to the rule. Tejeda is hoping to join their ranks, though it’s still a bit to be determined whether he does so on the mound or at the plate. He entered the spring as one of the most intriguing two-way talents in the class, and while some saw some regression, there are teams very intrigued by his upside. Many teams think Tejeda wants to hit, and he does have considerable raw power from both sides of the plate. Because of his long levers, however, there is length to his swing and a lot of zone to cover because of his size, leading to concern about his hit tool. While he’s not unathletic, he’d likely be limited to first base as he fills out. There is a lot of projection with him on the mound. Some have seen him up to 97 mph, but he’s typically in the 91-93 mph range, using his size to throw it with good plane. His changeup is his go-to secondary offering now, which he can sell decently out of his hand with some sink. His breaking ball is a slower slurve right now, but he could become a hard sinker-slider type in the future. He could have the chance to both pitch and hit at Florida, though most see his future as a pitcher.

The best prospect in this class from Montana, Blatter is a huge (6-foot-6) right-hander who went to the Draft League this summer in order to get seen more. It worked, as the Alabama recruit touched 95 mph with his fastball and showed off an 83-85 mph slider and slightly slower curve, all thrown with excellent spin rates.

Gatorade's Kansas Player of the Year, Lovich led Blue Valley West (Overland Park) to the state 6-A title by driving in six runs in the semifinals and earning the victory on the mound in the championship game. He's a 6-foot-4, 180-pounder with a quick right-handed stroke and a lot of projectable power, though his swing is naturally long, which leads to strikeouts. A high school shortstop who projects as a third baseman at the next level, he has committed to Missouri, where his brother Ross is an outfielder.

Louisiana State has a seriously stacked recruiting class lined up from the Class of 2022, including several members of MLB Pipeline’s Top 100 list. The pitching staff alone could include lefty Michael Kennedy from New York, right-hander Chase Shores from Texas and Noot, a big right-hander from the California prep ranks who showed off his stuff at showcase events like MLB’s PDP League, the Area Code Games and Perfect Game’s World Wood Bat Association World Championship last summer. He still lit up radar guns this spring but was a little more inconsistent. Noot has a big, durable, starting pitcher type of frame, with the 6-foot-4 right-hander reminding some of big league veteran Lance Lynn. While he’s a little bit of an upright thrower, he has an easy arm action and plenty of velocity now, with a fastball that’s typically in the 93-95 mph range. His two breaking balls can run into each other at times, but he does have two distinct pitches in a slower curve and firmer, tighter slider, and he had a pitch with a more cutter-like look to it at the Area Code Games. He has good feel to spin it, with some thinking he should focus on the slider, with it having the chance to be at least above-average in the future. He does have some feel for a changeup as well. As good as the stuff looks on paper, some scouts have been a little perplexed about why he hasn't been as dominant with it this spring. There might not be a ton of projection with Noot, as he’s close to maxed out body-wise now. But he does it with so little effort and as a decent athlete who has shown off good power as a hitter, there could be enough belief in his abilities translating to the pro game that teams will take him early enough to keep him from heading to Baton Rouge.

The main attraction at Stanford for scouts this spring has undoubtedly been outfielder Brock Jones, who has the chance to go on Day 1 of the Draft. If they were lucky, they also got to evaluate Mathews, a left-hander who has been very effective as a swingman/multi-inning reliever after showing off his stuff during a four-start stint in the Cape Cod League last summer. Tall and lanky, Mathews brings a four-pitch mix that plays up with funk and deception. He’ll throw his fastball anywhere in the 89- to 94-mph range, typically sitting around 91. His changeup is his best secondary offering, featuring two-plane fade while missing a lot of bats and getting ground-ball outs. His 80- to 83-mph slider has tight darting movement and he’ll fold in a slower 73- to 75-mph curve with three-quarter shape to it. While Mathews has a very healthy strikeout rate, he’s also struggled at times to find the strike zone, carrying a walk rate of around four per nine innings during his college career. A team taking him could send him out as a starter and seeing if he can refine his command, knowing that he understands how to get outs out of the bullpen. Whatever his role, he has a good chance of being just the third Stanford lefty to go in the top 10 rounds in the past five Drafts.

Every Draft has its share of pop-up prospects, players who came into the spring without much track record or history in front of scouts. Kelly might be Exhibit A in California for this category as he wasn’t really seen on the summer showcase circuit and was lightly recruited by colleges. But when the one-time quarterback at Tustin High School stopped playing football as a senior to focus on pitching, his stuff took off, leading to scouts running to Santa Ana, Calif., in a hurry. Kelly has already seen his velocity increase this year and it’s easy to dream on more to come, given his athleticism and 6-foot-4 frame. He’s been up to 93-94 mph at times this year, typically sitting a touch below that with his heater. But his calling card is really his plus curve. It’s one of the best breaking balls of any prep pitcher in the class, thrown with high spin rates, and he commands and manipulates it well. Having split his focus between two sports until this year, Kelly is a bit raw and has been inconsistent at times. But the combination of upside and athleticism is sure to excite teams in the top several rounds. He’ll stay local and head to UC Irvine if the Draft doesn’t work out.

Ortiz first jumped on the map as a 2022 Draft prospect at the Carlos Beltran Baseball Academy in Puerto Rico. He moved to Florida for better competition and more exposure at IMG Academy. Scouts who came in to see potential top-of-the-Draft outfielder Elijah Green also got to evaluate Ortiz, who has potential both in the infield and on the mound. Most believe Ortiz’s future is on the dirt, and all signs point to him preferring to be a position player at the next level. There’s no doubt he can play shortstop for a very long time, with a plus arm, good range and loose, easy actions. The bat is behind the glove, but he does have a loose and easy swing from the right side and he makes a lot of contact. A lack of physicality was a concern in terms of impacting the ball, but he had added some strength between the summer and early spring, even though his offensive performance tapered off as this season wore on. If the bat doesn’t come around, Ortiz has plenty of upside on the mound, with some scouts wondering if he’d be better off there to begin with. Without much instruction, or perceived desire to excel, he’s been up to 95 mph with his fastball, sitting 92-93 mph with ease while flashing an average slider and a little feel for a changeup. A team that signs him away from his Florida International commitment could send him out as a shortstop knowing the mound is a very viable Plan B.

Dickerson teamed with fellow outfielder Daylen Lile to lead Trinity HS (Louisville) to its first-ever Kentucky state championship in 2021, after which Lile signed with the Nationals for an over-slot $1.75 million in the second round. Scouts have returned to Trinity this spring to see Dickerson, who's similar athletically to Lile but not as advanced at the plate. He may not have a carrying tool to get paid now but the Louisville recruit has the potential to emerge as an early-round pick in 2025. Dickerson has dominated local prep competition but had some swing-and-miss issues on the showcase circuit last summer. His right-handed stroke is sound and he has the bat speed to catch up to velocity, so he should become at least an average hitter once he develops. The projectable strength and leverage in his 6-foot-2 frame give him similar power potential. Though Dickerson can post plus 60-yard dash times, his speed plays more as average on the diamond. As a result, he projects to move from center field in high school to an outfield corner in the long run. His solid arm strength should allow him to handle right field.

Charles has a good role model in his own family in terms of what the grind of pro ball can be like. His older brother, Art, was a first baseman taken in the 20th round of the 2010 Draft by the Blue Jays and has spent over a decade in the Minor Leagues, independent league ball and in Mexico. Austin has tools both on the mound and at the plate, and though he entered the spring without much track record with scouts, he has spent the spring showing off skills on both sides of the ball. Not your typical shortstop at 6-foot-6, Charles has shown off his plus athleticism and arm, along with solid actions, with some thinking he’ll fit well at third and is certainly athletic enough to play the outfield. Charles prefers to hit and he has some raw power to tap into. The competition he typically faces in the Bakersfield area isn’t great, but he did go off against Draft prospect Cutter Coffey in a recent matchup. Entering the spring, Charles hadn’t pitched much and was used mostly in relief early, but his role has grown, giving him more of a chance to show off a feel for pitching. Many scouts prefer him on the mound, as he’s up to 94 mph right now with more in the tank. He has decent feel for his slider and has a quick arm and delivery with deception. He could be a two-way player at Santa Barbara if he heads to college, but a team that likes toolsy players might be willing to let him hit, knowing the mound is a very solid backup plan.
Drew’s father, Kevin, was drafted in the 25th round by the White Sox in 1989. Conover made 18 appearances for Seton Hall this season, posting a 5.73 ERA with 43 strikeouts in 33 innings.

It can be tough to evaluate players in cold-weather states, and there’s no doubt that the climate wreaks havoc when scouting players from upstate New York. Despite the challenge, high school talents from the region like Joe Mack and Ian Anderson have landed in the first round. While there aren’t top-round candidates this year, a pair of prep arms from around Albany have popped onto radars, with lefty Michael Kennedy appealing to those who like pitchability and Van Kempen for those who love size and pure arm strength. Right now, Van Kempen is mostly a two-pitch power pitcher with considerable upside. He’s up to 95 mph with his fastball, a heater he throws with plus life, and he was 90-92 during his stint at the MLB Draft Combine. He complements it with a solid power curve, thrown in the upper 70s, that can miss a lot of bats. He does have a split-grip changeup and it isn’t bad, but he hasn’t needed it or thrown it much in high school competition. Van Kempen continues to grow into his 6-foot-6 frame, so there’s likely more consistent velocity to come, and he uses his size well on the mound. Still more thrower than pitcher, the West Virginia recruit is more of a projection prospect, but one who has considerable upside.

Pace is one of the fastest players in the Draft, but most scouts think his bat isn't ready for pro ball. He ran a 6.21-second 60-yard dash and showed some raw right-handed power in batting practice at the East Coast Pro showcase last summer, but his uphill swing doesn't produce a lot of results in game action. He'd be best off refining his hitting skills and center-field instincts at Michigan rather than signing now.

In his freshman year at Arizona State, Long hit .340 with 16 homers, placing him second, ahead of Barry Bonds, on the all-time freshman list and leaving scouts excited to see what he might do for an encore as a Draft-eligible sophomore this spring. It turned into a disappointing year at the plate as Long struggled to live up to expectations and was slowed by a wrist injury. Strong and physical, Long is a right-handed hitter with raw power to spare. It’s power-over-hit with some concern about swing-and-miss, though he does a good job of getting to his power when healthy. Long’s other standout tool has always been his plus arm that had been up to 97 mph in the past. He’s pitched a bit for the Sun Devils, but he’s focused on hitting since a sore shoulder kept him from potentially closing games. Long has worked hard on his defense and he’s improved at the hot corner, but there’s still work to be done to get him to average beyond that arm. It will be the power bat that gets him drafted and the team that takes him will think the 2021 version is the real Long.
